subject: Biology
topic: Plant Anatomy - Tissue Systems, Roots, Stems, Leaves

questions.question: What is the study of internal structure of plants called?
questions.explanation: Anatomy is the study of the internal structure of plants.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of plant organisation?
questions.explanation: Plants have cells as the basic unit, which are organised into tissues, and tissues into organs.

questions.question: How many types of tissue systems are present in plants based on structure and location?
questions.explanation: There are three types of tissue systems: epidermal, ground (fundamental), and vascular (conducting).

questions.question: Which tissue system forms the outermost covering of the whole plant body?
questions.explanation: The epidermal tissue system forms the outermost covering of the whole plant body.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the epidermal tissue system?
questions.explanation: The epidermal tissue system comprises epidermal cells, stomata, and epidermal appendages (trichomes and hairs). Parenchyma is part of ground tissue.

questions.question: Epidermis is usually:
questions.explanation: Epidermis is usually single-layered.

questions.question: Epidermal cells are:
questions.explanation: Epidermal cells are parenchymatous with a small amount of cytoplasm lining the cell wall and a large vacuole.

questions.question: The waxy thick layer covering the outside of epidermis is called:
questions.explanation: The outside of the epidermis is often covered with a waxy thick layer called the cuticle which prevents the loss of water.

questions.question: Cuticle is absent in:
questions.explanation: Cuticle is absent in roots.

questions.question: Stomata are structures present in the epidermis of:
questions.explanation: Stomata are structures present in the epidermis of leaves.

questions.question: What do stomata regulate?
questions.explanation: Stomata regulate the process of transpiration and gaseous exchange.

questions.question: Each stoma is composed of how many guard cells?
questions.explanation: Each stoma is composed of two bean-shaped cells known as guard cells which enclose stomatal pore.

questions.question: The shape of guard cells in grasses is:
questions.explanation: In grasses, the guard cells are dumb-bell shaped.

questions.question: In a typical stoma, which walls are highly thickened?

options.C: Both inner and outer walls equally

questions.explanation: The outer walls of guard cells (away from the stomatal pore) are thin and the inner walls (towards the stomatal pore) are highly thickened.

questions.question: Which organelle do guard cells possess that helps regulate stomatal opening?
questions.explanation: The guard cells possess chloroplasts and regulate the opening and closing of stomata.

questions.question: Specialised epidermal cells near guard cells that differ in shape and size are called:
questions.explanation: Sometimes, a few epidermal cells in the vicinity of the guard cells become specialised in their shape and size and are known as subsidiary cells.

questions.question: What is the stomatal apparatus?

options.C: Stomatal aperture, guard cells and surrounding subsidiary cells together

questions.explanation: The stomatal aperture, guard cells and the surrounding subsidiary cells are together called stomatal apparatus.

questions.question: Root hairs are:
questions.explanation: Root hairs are unicellular elongations of the epidermal cells and help absorb water and minerals from the soil.

questions.question: What is the function of root hairs?

options.B: Absorb water and minerals from soil

questions.explanation: Root hairs are unicellular elongations of the epidermal cells and help absorb water and minerals from the soil.

questions.question: Epidermal hairs on the stem are called:
questions.explanation: On the stem, the epidermal hairs are called trichomes.

questions.question: Trichomes in the shoot system are usually:
questions.explanation: The trichomes in the shoot system are usually multicellular.

questions.question: What is the function of trichomes?

options.C: Help in preventing water loss due to transpiration

questions.explanation: The trichomes help in preventing water loss due to transpiration.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about trichomes?

options.B: They may be branched or unbranched and soft or stiff
options.D: They are only found in roots

questions.explanation: Trichomes may be branched or unbranched and soft or stiff. They may even be secretory.

questions.question: Which tissue system constitutes the ground tissue?

options.C: All tissues except epidermis and vascular bundles

questions.explanation: All tissues except epidermis and vascular bundles constitute the ground tissue.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of ground tissue?
questions.explanation: Ground tissue consists of simple tissues such as parenchyma, collenchyma and sclerenchyma. Phloem is part of the vascular tissue system.

questions.question: Where are parenchymatous cells usually present in primary stems and roots?

options.B: Cortex, pericycle, pith and medullary rays

questions.explanation: Parenchymatous cells are usually present in cortex, pericycle, pith and medullary rays in the primary stems and roots.

questions.question: The ground tissue in leaves is called:
questions.explanation: In leaves, the ground tissue consists of thin-walled chloroplast containing cells and is called mesophyll.

questions.question: Which two complex tissues form the vascular system?
questions.explanation: The vascular system consists of complex tissues, the phloem and the xylem.

questions.question: In dicotyledonous stems, cambium is present:
questions.explanation: In dicotyledonous stems, cambium is present between phloem and xylem.

questions.question: Vascular bundles with cambium that can form secondary xylem and phloem are called:
questions.explanation: Such vascular bundles because of the presence of cambium possess the ability to form secondary xylem and phloem tissues, and hence are called open vascular bundles.

questions.question: Vascular bundles in monocotyledons are called closed because:

options.A: They are surrounded by a sheath
options.B: They have no cambium and do not form secondary tissues
options.C: The phloem is enclosed by xylem

questions.explanation: In monocotyledons, the vascular bundles have no cambium present in them. Hence, since they do not form secondary tissues they are referred to as closed.

questions.question: When xylem and phloem within a vascular bundle are arranged in an alternate manner along different radii, it is called:
questions.explanation: When xylem and phloem within a vascular bundle are arranged in an alternate manner along the different radii, the arrangement is called radial such as in roots.

questions.question: Radial arrangement of vascular bundles is found in:
questions.explanation: When xylem and phloem are arranged in an alternate manner along different radii, the arrangement is called radial such as in roots.

questions.question: In conjoint type of vascular bundles, xylem and phloem are:

options.B: Jointly situated along the same radius

questions.explanation: In conjoint type of vascular bundles, the xylem and phloem are jointly situated along the same radius of vascular bundles.

questions.question: Conjoint vascular bundles are common in:
questions.explanation: Such vascular bundles are common in stems and leaves.

questions.question: In conjoint vascular bundles, phloem is located:

options.A: On the inner side of xylem
options.C: Only on the outer side of xylem

questions.explanation: The conjoint vascular bundles usually have the phloem located only on the outer side of xylem.

questions.question: The outermost layer in a dicotyledonous root is:
questions.explanation: The outermost layer in a dicot root is epiblema.

questions.question: The cortex in dicot root consists of:

options.B: Several layers of thin-walled parenchyma cells with intercellular spaces

questions.explanation: The cortex consists of several layers of thin-walled parenchyma cells with intercellular spaces.

questions.question: The innermost layer of cortex in a dicot root is called:
questions.explanation: The innermost layer of the cortex is called endodermis.

questions.question: Endodermis comprises:

options.B: Single layer of barrel-shaped cells without any intercellular spaces

questions.explanation: Endodermis comprises a single layer of barrel-shaped cells without any intercellular spaces.

questions.question: Casparian strips are made of:
questions.explanation: The tangential as well as radial walls of the endodermal cells have a deposition of water-impermeable, waxy material suberin in the form of casparian strips.

questions.question: Casparian strips are found in:
questions.explanation: The tangential as well as radial walls of the endodermal cells have a deposition of suberin in the form of casparian strips.

questions.question: Pericycle consists of:

options.B: Few layers of thick-walled parenchyomatous cells

questions.explanation: Next to endodermis lies a few layers of thick-walled parenchyomatous cells referred to as pericycle.

questions.question: Which of the following takes place in pericycle cells of dicot root?

options.B: Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium during secondary growth

questions.explanation: Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium during the secondary growth takes place in pericycle cells.

questions.question: In a dicot root, the pith is:
questions.explanation: The pith is small or inconspicuous in a dicot root.

questions.question: Parenchymatous cells lying between xylem and phloem in a dicot root are called:
questions.explanation: The parenchymatous cells which lie between the xylem and the phloem are called conjuctive tissue.

questions.question: How many xylem and phloem patches are usually present in a dicot root?
questions.explanation: There are usually two to four xylem and phloem patches in a dicot root.

questions.question: The stele in a dicot root includes:

options.B: Pericycle, vascular bundles and pith (all tissues on innerside of endodermis)

questions.explanation: All tissues on the innerside of the endodermis such as pericycle, vascular bundles and pith constitute the stele.

questions.question: Compared to dicot root, monocot root has:
questions.explanation: As compared to the dicot root which have fewer xylem bundles, there are usually more than six (polyarch) xylem bundles in the monocot root.

questions.question: The pith in a monocot root is:
questions.explanation: Pith is large and well developed in monocot root.

questions.question: Monocotyledonous roots undergo:
questions.explanation: Monocotyledonous roots do not undergo any secondary growth.

questions.question: The outermost protective layer of the dicotyledonous stem is:
questions.explanation: The transverse section of a typical young dicotyledonous stem shows that the epidermis is the outermost protective layer of the stem.

questions.question: The epidermis of dicot stem is covered with:
questions.explanation: Covered with a thin layer of cuticle, it may bear trichomes and a few stomata.

questions.question: The outer part of cortex in dicot stem that provides mechanical strength is called:
questions.explanation: The outer hypodermis, consists of a few layers of collenchymatous cells just below the epidermis, which provide mechanical strength to the young stem.

questions.question: The hypodermis in a dicot stem consists of:
questions.explanation: The outer hypodermis consists of a few layers of collenchymatous cells just below the epidermis.

questions.question: Cortical layers below hypodermis in dicot stem consist of:

options.B: Rounded thin walled parenchymatous cells with conspicuous intercellular spaces

questions.explanation: Cortical layers below hypodermis consist of rounded thin walled parenchymatous cells with conspicuous intercellular spaces.

questions.question: The endodermis of a dicot stem is also called:
questions.explanation: The cells of the endodermis are rich in starch grains and the layer is also referred to as the starch sheath.

questions.question: In dicot stem, pericycle is present as:

options.B: Semi-lunar patches of sclerenchyma above phloem

questions.explanation: Pericycle is present on the inner side of the endodermis and above the phloem in the form of semi-lunar patches of sclerenchyma.

questions.question: Medullary rays in dicot stem are composed of:

options.B: Few layers of radially placed parenchymatous cells between vascular bundles

questions.explanation: In between the vascular bundles there are a few layers of radially placed parenchymatous cells, which constitute medullary rays.

questions.question: The arrangement of vascular bundles in dicot stem is:
questions.explanation: A large number of vascular bundles are arranged in a ring; the 'ring' arrangement of vascular bundles is a characteristic of dicot stem.

questions.question: The 'ring' arrangement of vascular bundles is a characteristic of:
questions.explanation: The 'ring' arrangement of vascular bundles is a characteristic of dicot stem.

questions.question: Each vascular bundle in dicot stem is:
questions.explanation: Each vascular bundle is conjoint, open, and with endarch protoxylem.

questions.question: The pith in dicot stem consists of:

options.B: Large number of rounded parenchymatous cells with large intercellular spaces

questions.explanation: A large number of rounded, parenchymatous cells with large intercellular spaces which occupy the central portion of the stem constitute the pith.

questions.question: The hypodermis of monocot stem is:
questions.explanation: The monocot stem has a sclerenchymatous hypodermis.

questions.question: Vascular bundles in monocot stem are:
questions.explanation: The monocot stem has a large number of scattered vascular bundles.

questions.question: Each vascular bundle in monocot stem is surrounded by:
questions.explanation: The monocot stem has a large number of scattered vascular bundles, each surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheath.

questions.question: The ground tissue in monocot stem is:
questions.explanation: The monocot stem has a large, conspicuous parenchymatous ground tissue.

questions.question: Vascular bundles in monocot stem are:
questions.explanation: Vascular bundles in monocot stem are conjoint and closed.

questions.question: In monocot stem, peripheral vascular bundles compared to centrally located ones are:
questions.explanation: Peripheral vascular bundles are generally smaller than the centrally located ones in monocot stem.

questions.question: Phloem parenchyma is absent in:
questions.explanation: The phloem parenchyma is absent in monocot stem.

questions.question: Water-containing cavities are present within the vascular bundles of:
questions.explanation: Water-containing cavities are present within the vascular bundles of monocot stem.

questions.question: A dorsiventral leaf belongs to which group?
questions.explanation: Dorsiventral leaves belong to dicotyledonous plants.

questions.question: Vertical section of a dorsiventral leaf shows how many main parts?
questions.explanation: The vertical section of a dorsiventral leaf through the lamina shows three main parts, namely, epidermis, mesophyll and vascular system.

questions.question: The upper surface of a leaf is called:
questions.explanation: The epidermis which covers the upper surface of the leaf is the adaxial epidermis.

questions.question: The lower surface of a leaf is called:
questions.explanation: The epidermis which covers the lower surface of the leaf is the abaxial epidermis.

questions.question: Which surface of a dicot leaf generally bears more stomata?
questions.explanation: The abaxial epidermis generally bears more stomata than the adaxial epidermis.

questions.question: The tissue between upper and lower epidermis of a leaf is called:
questions.explanation: The tissue between the upper and the lower epidermis is called the mesophyll.

questions.question: Mesophyll is made up of:
questions.explanation: Mesophyll, which possesses chloroplasts and carry out photosynthesis, is made up of parenchyma.

questions.question: Mesophyll has how many types of cells?
questions.explanation: Mesophyll has two types of cells – the palisade parenchyma and the spongy parenchyma.

questions.question: Palisade parenchyma is placed:
questions.explanation: The adaxially placed palisade parenchyma is made up of elongated cells, which are arranged vertically and parallel to each other.

questions.question: Palisade parenchyma cells are arranged:

options.B: Vertically and parallel to each other

questions.explanation: The adaxially placed palisade parenchyma is made up of elongated cells, which are arranged vertically and parallel to each other.

questions.question: Spongy parenchyma is situated:

options.A: Above palisade cells extending to upper epidermis
options.B: Below palisade cells extending to lower epidermis

questions.explanation: The oval or round and loosely arranged spongy parenchyma is situated below the palisade cells and extends to the lower epidermis.

questions.question: Between spongy parenchyma cells there are:

options.C: Numerous large spaces and air cavities

questions.explanation: There are numerous large spaces and air cavities between spongy parenchyma cells.

questions.question: Vascular bundles in dicot leaves are surrounded by:

options.B: A layer of thick walled bundle sheath cells

questions.explanation: The vascular bundles are surrounded by a layer of thick walled bundle sheath cells.

questions.question: The venation pattern in dicot leaves is:
questions.explanation: The veins vary in thickness in the reticulate venation of the dicot leaves.

questions.question: An isobilateral leaf belongs to which group?
questions.explanation: Isobilateral leaf is the monocotyledonous leaf.

questions.question: In an isobilateral leaf, stomata are present:

options.C: On both surfaces of the epidermis

questions.explanation: In an isobilateral leaf, the stomata are present on both the surfaces of the epidermis.

questions.question: In an isobilateral (monocot) leaf, the mesophyll is:

options.A: Differentiated into palisade and spongy parenchyma
options.B: Not differentiated into palisade and spongy parenchyma

questions.explanation: In isobilateral leaves, the mesophyll is not differentiated into palisade and spongy parenchyma.

questions.question: Bulliform cells are found in:
questions.explanation: In grasses, certain adaxial epidermal cells along the veins modify themselves into large, empty, colourless cells. These are called bulliform cells.

questions.question: Bulliform cells are:
questions.explanation: Certain adaxial epidermal cells along the veins modify themselves into large, empty, colourless cells. These are called bulliform cells.

questions.question: When bulliform cells are turgid (full of water):
questions.explanation: When the bulliform cells in the leaves have absorbed water and are turgid, the leaf surface is exposed.

questions.question: When bulliform cells are flaccid (due to water stress):

options.B: Leaves curl inwards to minimise water loss

questions.explanation: When they are flaccid due to water stress, they make the leaves curl inwards to minimise water loss.

questions.question: The venation pattern in monocot leaves is:
questions.explanation: The parallel venation in monocot leaves is reflected in the near similar sizes of vascular bundles.

questions.question: Plant tissues are broadly classified into:
questions.explanation: The plant tissues are broadly classified into meristematic (apical, lateral and intercalary) and permanent (simple and complex).

questions.question: Meristematic tissues are of how many types?
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissues are of three types - apical, lateral and intercalary.

questions.question: Which of the following is a main function of tissues in plants?

options.B: Assimilation and storage of food, transportation and mechanical support

questions.explanation: Assimilation of food and its storage, transportation of water, minerals and photosynthates, and mechanical support are the main functions of tissues.

questions.question: Secondary growth occurs in most of the:
questions.explanation: The secondary growth occurs in most of the dicotyledonous roots and stems.

questions.question: Monocots and dicots differ in:

options.B: Type, number and location of vascular bundles
options.C: Only the number of vascular bundles
options.D: Only the location of vascular bundles

questions.explanation: Monocotyledonous and dicotyledonous plants show marked variation in their internal structures. They differ in type, number and location of vascular bundles.

questions.question: The ground tissue system is divided into how many zones?
questions.explanation: The ground tissue system forms the main bulk of the plant. It is divided into three zones – cortex, pericycle and pith.

questions.question: The vascular tissue system is formed by:
questions.explanation: The vascular tissue system is formed by the xylem and phloem.

questions.question: Which plant showed as example for dicot root in the chapter?
questions.explanation: Figure 6.3(a) shows the transverse section of the sunflower root as an example of dicot root.

questions.question: Vascular bundles translocate:
questions.explanation: The vascular bundles form the conducting tissue and translocate water, minerals and food material.

questions.question: Which of the following structures is a characteristic feature specifically of dicot stem (NOT monocot stem)?
questions.explanation: The 'ring' arrangement of vascular bundles is a characteristic of dicot stem. Monocot stem has scattered vascular bundles.

questions.question: In which type of root does a cambium ring develop between xylem and phloem?
questions.explanation: Later, a cambium ring develops between the xylem and phloem in dicot root.

questions.question: The adaxial epidermis of dicot leaf may:
questions.explanation: The abaxial epidermis generally bears more stomata than the adaxial epidermis. The latter (adaxial) may even lack stomata.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly differentiates monocot stem from dicot stem?

options.A: Monocot has collenchymatous hypodermis; dicot has sclerenchymatous
options.B: Monocot has ring arrangement; dicot has scattered vascular bundles
options.C: Monocot has scattered vascular bundles; dicot has ring arrangement
options.D: Monocot has open vascular bundles; dicot has closed vascular bundles

questions.explanation: Monocot stem has scattered vascular bundles while dicot stem has ring arrangement of vascular bundles.

questions.question: In leaves, the function of mesophyll is:
questions.explanation: Mesophyll possesses chloroplasts and carry out photosynthesis.

questions.question: The protoxylem in dicot stem is:
questions.explanation: Each vascular bundle in dicot stem is conjoint, open, and with endarch protoxylem.

questions.question: Which of the following is found in both monocot and dicot root?

options.D: Epidermis, cortex, endodermis, pericycle, vascular bundles and pith

questions.explanation: The anatomy of the monocot root is similar to the dicot root in many respects. It has epidermis, cortex, endodermis, pericycle, vascular bundles and pith.

questions.question: The monocot leaf is also called:
questions.explanation: The monocot leaf is called isobilateral leaf because stomata are present on both the surfaces.

questions.question: Which cells in grasses help in rolling/unrolling of leaves according to water availability?
questions.explanation: Bulliform cells help in rolling and unrolling of grass leaves. When turgid, leaf surface is exposed; when flaccid, leaves curl inwards.

questions.question: The size of vascular bundles in dicot leaves depends on:
questions.explanation: The size of the vascular bundles are dependent on the size of the veins.

questions.question: The main bulk of the plant is formed by:
questions.explanation: The ground tissue system forms the main bulk of the plant.

questions.question: The cortex in dicot stem has how many sub-zones?
questions.explanation: The cortex in dicot stem consists of three sub-zones: hypodermis, cortical layers (parenchyma), and endodermis.

questions.question: What is the significance of Casparian strips?

options.B: Made of water-impermeable waxy material suberin, regulate water movement

questions.explanation: The tangential as well as radial walls of the endodermal cells have a deposition of water-impermeable, waxy material suberin in the form of casparian strips.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about epidermal cells?

options.A: They have large cytoplasm and small vacuole
options.B: They have a small amount of cytoplasm and a large vacuole

questions.explanation: Epidermal cells are parenchymatous with a small amount of cytoplasm lining the cell wall and a large vacuole.

questions.question: Open vascular bundles can form secondary tissues because:

options.A: They have xylem on the outside
options.B: They have cambium between phloem and xylem

questions.explanation: Such vascular bundles because of the presence of cambium possess the ability to form secondary xylem and phloem tissues.

questions.question: Which of the following parts of a flowering plant lacks stomata?
questions.explanation: Cuticle and also stomata are absent in roots. Stomata regulate transpiration which roots don't need.

questions.question: In monocot leaves, vascular bundles (except main veins) are of:
questions.explanation: The parallel venation in monocot leaves is reflected in the near similar sizes of vascular bundles (except in main veins).

questions.question: Permanent plant tissues are classified as:
questions.explanation: Plant tissues are broadly classified into meristematic and permanent (simple and complex).

questions.question: Which of the following is a feature of closed vascular bundles?
questions.explanation: Monocotyledon vascular bundles have no cambium present in them and are referred to as closed.

questions.question: In a dicot leaf, which layer may even lack stomata?
questions.explanation: The abaxial epidermis generally bears more stomata than the adaxial epidermis. The latter (adaxial) may even lack stomata.

questions.question: Which type of cells does the endodermis of a dicot root comprise?
questions.explanation: Endodermis comprises a single layer of barrel-shaped cells without any intercellular spaces.

questions.question: Which structure in the leaf is visible in the veins and the midrib?
questions.explanation: Vascular system includes vascular bundles, which can be seen in the veins and the midrib.

questions.question: The word 'polyarch' in context of monocot root refers to:
questions.explanation: There are usually more than six (polyarch) xylem bundles in the monocot root.

questions.question: Conjunctive tissue in dicot root consists of:

options.A: Sclerenchymatous cells between xylem and phloem
options.B: Parenchymatous cells between xylem and phloem
options.C: Collenchymatous cells between xylem and phloem
options.D: Meristematic cells between xylem and phloem

questions.explanation: The parenchymatous cells which lie between the xylem and the phloem are called conjuctive tissue.

questions.question: The epidermal tissue system of the whole plant body is formed by:
questions.explanation: The epidermal tissue system forms the outer-most covering of the whole plant body and comprises epidermal cells, stomata and the epidermal appendages – the trichomes and hairs.

questions.question: In monocot leaves, bulliform cells are found on which surface?
questions.explanation: Certain adaxial epidermal cells along the veins modify themselves into bulliform cells in grasses.

questions.question: What happens to grass leaves when bulliform cells lose water?

options.B: Leaves curl inwards to minimise water loss

questions.explanation: When they are flaccid due to water stress, they make the leaves curl inwards to minimise water loss.

questions.question: The cortex of monocot stem is part of which tissue system?
questions.explanation: The ground tissue system includes all tissues except epidermis and vascular bundles, so cortex is part of ground tissue system.

questions.question: Guard cells regulate:
questions.explanation: The guard cells possess chloroplasts and regulate the opening and closing of stomata.

questions.question: Bean-shaped guard cells are found in:
questions.explanation: Each stoma is composed of two bean-shaped cells known as guard cells. In grasses specifically, the guard cells are dumb-bell shaped.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the cuticle?

options.A: A thin cellulosic layer inside the epidermis
options.B: A waxy thick layer outside the epidermis
options.C: A protein layer between epidermis and cortex
options.D: A pectin layer within cell walls

questions.explanation: The outside of the epidermis is often covered with a waxy thick layer called the cuticle which prevents the loss of water.

questions.question: The anatomy of monocot root compared to dicot root is:

options.D: Only different in the number of epidermis layers

questions.explanation: The anatomy of the monocot root is similar to the dicot root in many respects.

questions.question: Which tissue separates cortex from the stele in roots?
questions.explanation: The endodermis is the innermost layer of cortex and separates the cortex from the stele.

questions.question: Internal structures of plants also show adaptations to:
questions.explanation: Internal structures also show adaptations to diverse environments.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct sequence from outside to inside in a dicot root?

options.B: Epiblema → Cortex → Endodermis → Pericycle → Vascular bundles/Pith

questions.explanation: The correct sequence from outside to inside in dicot root is: Epiblema → Cortex → Endodermis → Pericycle → Vascular bundles and Pith (stele).

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct sequence from outside to inside in a dicot stem?

options.A: Epidermis → Endodermis → Hypodermis → Pericycle → Vascular bundles → Pith
options.B: Epidermis → Hypodermis → Cortical parenchyma → Endodermis → Pericycle → Vascular bundles → Pith
options.C: Epidermis → Cortex → Pith → Vascular bundles → Pericycle

questions.explanation: The correct sequence from outside to inside in dicot stem: Epidermis → Hypodermis → Cortical parenchyma → Endodermis (starch sheath) → Pericycle → Vascular bundles → Pith.

questions.question: Which part of the monocot stem is a large conspicuous parenchymatous mass?
questions.explanation: The monocot stem has a large, conspicuous parenchymatous ground tissue.

questions.question: What type of mechanical tissue is the hypodermis in dicot stem?
questions.explanation: The outer hypodermis consists of a few layers of collenchymatous cells just below the epidermis, which provide mechanical strength to the young stem.

questions.question: Which type of mechanical tissue is the hypodermis in monocot stem?
questions.explanation: The monocot stem has a sclerenchymatous hypodermis.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the function of vascular bundles?

options.B: Form conducting tissue and translocate water, minerals and food material

questions.explanation: The vascular bundles form the conducting tissue and translocate water, minerals and food material.

questions.question: Palisade parenchyma cells are:

options.A: Oval or round and loosely arranged

questions.explanation: The adaxially placed palisade parenchyma is made up of elongated cells, which are arranged vertically and parallel to each other.

questions.question: Spongy parenchyma cells are:

options.B: Oval or round and loosely arranged

questions.explanation: The oval or round and loosely arranged spongy parenchyma is situated below the palisade cells.

questions.question: Which tissue in the dicot leaf is adapted for maximum light absorption for photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: The adaxially placed palisade parenchyma with elongated cells arranged vertically and parallel to each other is adapted for maximum light absorption for photosynthesis.

questions.question: The air cavities in spongy mesophyll are important for:
questions.explanation: There are numerous large spaces and air cavities between spongy parenchyma cells which facilitate gaseous exchange.

questions.question: In the ground tissue of the dicot leaf (mesophyll), which organelle is present?
questions.explanation: In leaves, the ground tissue consists of thin-walled chloroplast containing cells and is called mesophyll.

questions.question: The midrib of a leaf contains:
questions.explanation: Vascular system includes vascular bundles, which can be seen in the veins and the midrib.

questions.question: Which of these is the correct match for types of vascular bundles shown in Figure 6.2?
questions.explanation: Figure 6.2 shows: (a) radial (b) conjoint closed (c) conjoint open vascular bundles.

questions.question: Monocot and dicot stems differ in the arrangement of which structure?
questions.explanation: The key difference between monocot and dicot stem is the arrangement of vascular bundles — scattered in monocot, ring arrangement in dicot.

questions.question: Subsidiary cells are found in the:

options.B: Vicinity of guard cells in epidermis

questions.explanation: Sometimes, a few epidermal cells in the vicinity of the guard cells become specialised in their shape and size and are known as subsidiary cells.

questions.question: The epidermal appendages include:
questions.explanation: The epidermal appendages are the trichomes and hairs.

questions.question: Which of the following is common to both monocot and dicot root?
questions.explanation: Both monocot and dicot roots have endodermis with Casparian strips made of suberin.

questions.question: Secondary growth is absent in:
questions.explanation: Monocotyledonous roots do not undergo any secondary growth.

questions.question: In dicot stem, which part of pericycle appears above phloem?
questions.explanation: Pericycle is present on the inner side of the endodermis and above the phloem in the form of semi-lunar patches of sclerenchyma.

questions.question: The stele of a root includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: All tissues on the innerside of the endodermis such as pericycle, vascular bundles and pith constitute the stele. Cortex is outside the endodermis.

questions.question: The function of cuticle on epidermis is:
questions.explanation: The cuticle prevents the loss of water.

questions.question: The main difference between monocot and dicot leaf (isobilateral vs dorsiventral) is that in isobilateral leaf:

options.A: Stomata only on lower surface, mesophyll differentiated
options.B: Stomata on both surfaces, mesophyll not differentiated into palisade and spongy
options.D: Stomata only on upper surface, no mesophyll differentiation

questions.explanation: In isobilateral leaf, stomata are present on both surfaces of the epidermis; and the mesophyll is not differentiated into palisade and spongy parenchyma.

questions.question: Inner walls of guard cells (towards stomatal pore) are:
questions.explanation: The inner walls (towards the stomatal pore) are highly thickened.

questions.question: Outer walls of guard cells (away from stomatal pore) are:
questions.explanation: The outer walls of guard cells (away from the stomatal pore) are thin.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature that distinguishes a monocot stem transverse section from a dicot stem?

options.B: Scattered vascular bundles in parenchymatous ground tissue

questions.explanation: Monocot stem has scattered vascular bundles in large parenchymatous ground tissue unlike dicot stem where vascular bundles are in a ring.

questions.question: Lateral roots originate from:
questions.explanation: Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium during the secondary growth takes place in pericycle cells.

questions.question: Trichomes may function as:
questions.explanation: Trichomes may even be secretory.

questions.question: The sub-stomatal cavity is found below:

options.A: Guard cells on the upper surface of dicot leaf
options.B: The stomata on the abaxial surface of leaves

questions.explanation: Sub-stomatal cavity is present below the stomata, especially on the abaxial surface in leaves (as shown in Figure 6.5).

questions.question: The ground tissue of dicot leaf is called mesophyll which is made of parenchyma. This mesophyll is responsible for:
questions.explanation: Mesophyll, which possesses chloroplasts and carry out photosynthesis, is made up of parenchyma.

questions.question: The three tissue systems in flowering plants are:
questions.explanation: There are three types of tissue systems – epidermal, ground and vascular.

questions.question: The conducting tissue system is formed by:
questions.explanation: The vascular tissue system is formed by the xylem and phloem.

questions.question: What is the primary function of pericycle in roots?

options.B: Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium

questions.explanation: Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium during the secondary growth takes place in the pericycle cells.

questions.question: What type of venation is characteristic of dicot leaves?
questions.explanation: The veins vary in thickness in the reticulate venation of the dicot leaves.

questions.question: What type of venation is characteristic of monocot leaves?
questions.explanation: The parallel venation in monocot leaves is reflected in the near similar sizes of vascular bundles.

questions.question: The cuticle on leaf epidermis is:
questions.explanation: The epidermis which covers both the upper and lower surface of the leaf has a conspicuous cuticle.

questions.question: Epidermal cells form a:
questions.explanation: Epidermis is made up of elongated, compactly arranged cells, which form a continuous layer.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of the epidermal tissue system?
questions.explanation: Xylem vessels are part of the vascular tissue system, not the epidermal tissue system.

questions.question: In the dicot root, a cambium ring develops between xylem and phloem. This marks the beginning of:
questions.explanation: Later, a cambium ring develops between the xylem and phloem in the dicot root, which is associated with secondary growth.

questions.question: Epidermal cells are compactly arranged. This means they have:
questions.explanation: Epidermal cells are elongated and compactly arranged, forming a continuous layer, meaning no intercellular spaces.

questions.question: In a young dicot stem section, what can be found just below the epidermis?
questions.explanation: The outer hypodermis consists of a few layers of collenchymatous cells just below the epidermis.

questions.question: How do monocot and dicot roots differ in their xylem?

options.A: Dicot has polyarch, monocot has fewer xylem bundles
options.B: Monocot has polyarch (more than 6), dicot has fewer (2-4)
options.C: Both have same number of xylem bundles

questions.explanation: As compared to the dicot root which have fewer xylem bundles (usually 2-4), there are usually more than six (polyarch) xylem bundles in the monocot root.

questions.question: The epidermal appendages are:
questions.explanation: The epidermal appendages are the trichomes and hairs.

questions.question: Endodermis in dicot stem is also the innermost layer of:
questions.explanation: The innermost layer of the cortex in dicot stem is called the endodermis.

questions.question: What does the stele of a dicot root include?
questions.explanation: All tissues on the innerside of the endodermis such as pericycle, vascular bundles and pith constitute the stele.

questions.question: In isobilateral leaf, the mesophyll is:

options.A: Differentiated into palisade above and spongy below

questions.explanation: In an isobilateral (monocot) leaf, the mesophyll is not differentiated into palisade and spongy parenchyma.

questions.question: Both monocot and dicot leaves have which common feature?
questions.explanation: Both monocot and dicot leaves have bundle sheath surrounding vascular bundles.

questions.question: The ground tissue system forms the main bulk of the plant and is divided into three zones. What are they?
questions.explanation: The ground tissue system is divided into three zones – cortex, pericycle and pith.

questions.question: Which of the following is a xerophytic adaptation in monocot grass leaves?

options.B: Bulliform cells that allow leaf rolling to minimize water loss

questions.explanation: Bulliform cells in grass leaves allow rolling inwards when flaccid, minimising water loss — a xerophytic adaptation.

questions.question: Medullary rays are present in:
questions.explanation: In between the vascular bundles in dicot stem there are a few layers of radially placed parenchymatous cells, which constitute medullary rays.

questions.question: Which of the following lacks secondary growth?
questions.explanation: Monocotyledonous roots and stems do not undergo secondary growth because their vascular bundles lack cambium.

questions.question: The ability to form secondary xylem and phloem depends on the presence of:
questions.explanation: Such vascular bundles because of the presence of cambium possess the ability to form secondary xylem and phloem tissues.

questions.question: In a transverse section, if a plant material shows conjoint, scattered vascular bundles surrounded by sclerenchymatous bundle sheaths and absent phloem parenchyma, it is identified as:
questions.explanation: This exactly describes the monocot stem: scattered, conjoint, closed vascular bundles with sclerenchymatous bundle sheath, absent phloem parenchyma.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the dicot leaf (dorsiventral) vs monocot leaf (isobilateral)?

options.A: Dorsiventral: stomata on both surfaces; Isobilateral: stomata only on lower surface
options.B: Dorsiventral: mesophyll undifferentiated; Isobilateral: mesophyll differentiated
options.C: Dorsiventral: more stomata on lower surface, differentiated mesophyll; Isobilateral: stomata on both surfaces, undifferentiated mesophyll
options.D: Both are identical in all ways

questions.explanation: Dorsiventral leaf has more stomata on abaxial surface, with differentiated palisade and spongy mesophyll. Isobilateral leaf has stomata on both surfaces and undifferentiated mesophyll.

questions.question: In a dicot stem, vascular bundles are arranged:
questions.explanation: A large number of vascular bundles are arranged in a ring; the 'ring' arrangement of vascular bundles is a characteristic of dicot stem.

questions.question: The cells of the epiblema in dicot root that protrude as root hairs are:
questions.explanation: Many of the cells of epiblema protrude in the form of unicellular root hairs.

questions.question: The adaxial epidermis of a dicot leaf is on the:
questions.explanation: The epidermis which covers the upper surface (adaxial epidermis) has a conspicuous cuticle.

questions.question: In monocot stem, which vascular bundles are larger — peripheral or central?
questions.explanation: Peripheral vascular bundles are generally smaller than the centrally located ones in monocot stem.

questions.question: An epidermal cell typically has:

options.A: A large nucleus and small vacuole
options.B: Small cytoplasm lining cell wall and large vacuole

questions.explanation: Epidermal cells are parenchymatous with a small amount of cytoplasm lining the cell wall and a large vacuole.

questions.question: The vascular bundle type found in roots is:
questions.explanation: When xylem and phloem are arranged in an alternate manner along different radii, the arrangement is called radial, such as in roots.

questions.question: The vascular bundle type found in stems and leaves is:
questions.explanation: In conjoint type of vascular bundles, the xylem and phloem are jointly situated along the same radius. Such vascular bundles are common in stems and leaves.

questions.question: In a conjoint vascular bundle, phloem is typically on the:
questions.explanation: The conjoint vascular bundles usually have the phloem located only on the outer side of xylem.

questions.question: The term 'open' in open vascular bundle refers to the presence of:
questions.explanation: Open vascular bundles have cambium present between phloem and xylem, enabling formation of secondary tissues.

questions.question: The term 'closed' in closed vascular bundle means:

options.B: No cambium, so secondary growth is closed/impossible

questions.explanation: Closed vascular bundles have no cambium, hence they do not form secondary tissues.

questions.question: Chapter 6 is specifically about the internal structure of which type of plants?
questions.explanation: Chapter 6 is about the internal structure and functional organisation of higher plants (flowering plants/angiosperms).

questions.question: Which of the following is correct about epidermal cells in leaves?

options.B: Guard cells have chloroplasts but ordinary epidermal cells typically do not

questions.explanation: Guard cells possess chloroplasts. Ordinary epidermal cells are parenchymatous and generally lack chloroplasts.

questions.question: The pericycle in a dicot root is positioned:

options.C: Next to endodermis, towards the centre

questions.explanation: Next to endodermis lies a few layers of thick-walled parenchyomatous cells referred to as pericycle.

questions.question: Medullary rays act as:

options.B: Radially placed parenchymatous cells between vascular bundles in stem

questions.explanation: In between the vascular bundles there are a few layers of radially placed parenchymatous cells, which constitute medullary rays.

questions.question: In which organ does the stele contain more than six xylem bundles alternating with phloem?
questions.explanation: There are usually more than six (polyarch) xylem bundles in the monocot root.

questions.question: Which feature is common to the epidermal tissue of both root and shoot systems?
questions.explanation: Epidermal cells are present in both root and shoot epidermal tissue. Cuticle and stomata are absent in roots; root has root hairs not trichomes.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct statement about trichomes?

options.A: They are unicellular in shoot system
options.C: They may be branched or unbranched, soft or stiff, or even secretory

questions.explanation: The trichomes in the shoot system may be branched or unbranched and soft or stiff. They may even be secretory.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT found in an isobilateral leaf?
questions.explanation: In isobilateral leaf, the mesophyll is NOT differentiated into palisade and spongy parenchyma.

questions.question: Which of the following features is ONLY found in grasses among monocot leaves?
questions.explanation: In grasses, certain adaxial epidermal cells along the veins modify themselves into large, empty, colourless bulliform cells.

questions.question: What is the role of ground tissue in leaves compared to other parts of the plant?

options.B: In leaves it is called mesophyll and carries out photosynthesis
options.C: In leaves it provides mechanical support only

questions.explanation: In leaves, the ground tissue consists of thin-walled chloroplast containing cells and is called mesophyll — its role is photosynthesis.

questions.question: In the endodermis, casparian strips are found on which walls?

options.C: Tangential as well as radial walls

questions.explanation: The tangential as well as radial walls of the endodermal cells have a deposition of water-impermeable, waxy material suberin in the form of casparian strips.

questions.question: Root hairs are elongations of cells of the:
questions.explanation: Root hairs are unicellular elongations of the epidermal cells (epiblema cells in roots).

questions.question: The palisade parenchyma is on the adaxial side. 'Adaxial' means:
questions.explanation: Adaxial means towards the axis — this is the upper surface of the leaf.

questions.question: Abaxial means:
questions.explanation: Abaxial means away from the axis — this is the lower surface of the leaf.

questions.question: Which of the following is correct regarding the guard cells in grasses?
questions.explanation: In grasses, the guard cells are dumb-bell shaped.

questions.question: The endodermis of dicot root has a special material in its cell walls. What is it called?
questions.explanation: The tangential as well as radial walls of the endodermal cells have a deposition of water-impermeable, waxy material suberin in the form of casparian strips.

questions.question: Spongy parenchyma extends from:

options.B: Below palisade cells to lower epidermis

questions.explanation: The spongy parenchyma is situated below the palisade cells and extends to the lower epidermis.

questions.question: Which tissue in dicot stem has cells rich in starch grains?
questions.explanation: The cells of the endodermis are rich in starch grains and the layer is also referred to as the starch sheath.

questions.question: For the study of tissue organisation of roots, stems and leaves, sections are taken at:
questions.explanation: For a better understanding of tissue organisation of roots, stems and leaves, it is convenient to study the transverse sections of the mature zones of these organs.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT belong to the vascular tissue system?
questions.explanation: Parenchyma of cortex belongs to the ground tissue system, not the vascular tissue system.

questions.question: In dicot root, vascular cambium arises from:
questions.explanation: Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium during the secondary growth takes place in pericycle cells.

questions.question: How many main layers are visible in a transverse section of dorsiventral (dicot) leaf?
questions.explanation: The vertical section of a dorsiventral leaf through the lamina shows three main parts, namely, epidermis, mesophyll and vascular system.

questions.question: What determines the size of vascular bundles in leaf veins?
questions.explanation: The size of the vascular bundles are dependent on the size of the veins.

questions.question: In which structure of the plant body does cortex exist?
questions.explanation: Cortex is part of ground tissue system found in both roots and stems as a region between epidermis/epiblema and endodermis.

questions.question: Vascular bundles in monocot root are described as:

options.B: Radial with more than 6 xylem bundles

questions.explanation: In monocot root, xylem and phloem alternate (radial) and there are usually more than six (polyarch) xylem bundles.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of sclerenchymatous bundle sheath in monocot stem?

options.A: It surrounds each individual vascular bundle
options.C: It is found only in the pith

questions.explanation: The monocot stem has scattered vascular bundles, each surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheath.

questions.question: Which of the following summarises why the monocot vascular bundle is called 'closed'?

options.A: It is enclosed by a thick sheath preventing entry of substances
options.B: Absence of cambium means no secondary growth is possible
options.C: The phloem is enclosed by xylem
options.D: Both xylem and phloem are sealed within the bundle

questions.explanation: In monocotyledons, the vascular bundles have no cambium. Hence, since they do not form secondary tissues they are referred to as closed.

questions.question: Which of the following is a direct function of stomata?
questions.explanation: Stomata regulate the process of transpiration and gaseous exchange.

questions.question: The cells of the cortex in dicot root are described as:

options.B: Thin-walled parenchyma cells with intercellular spaces

questions.explanation: The cortex consists of several layers of thin-walled parenchyma cells with intercellular spaces.

questions.question: What is the key structural difference in epidermal hair between root and shoot?

options.A: Root hairs are multicellular; shoot trichomes are unicellular
options.B: Root hairs are unicellular; shoot trichomes are usually multicellular

questions.explanation: Root hairs are unicellular elongations. The trichomes in the shoot system are usually multicellular.

questions.question: Which cells in the stomatal apparatus contain chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: The guard cells possess chloroplasts and regulate the opening and closing of stomata.

questions.question: The presence of collenchymatous hypodermis in dicot stem serves to:

options.B: Provide mechanical strength to the young stem

questions.explanation: The outer hypodermis, consists of a few layers of collenchymatous cells just below the epidermis, which provide mechanical strength to the young stem.

questions.question: The conjunctive tissue in dicot root is located:
questions.explanation: The parenchymatous cells which lie between the xylem and the phloem are called conjuctive tissue.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about dicot root vs monocot root?

options.A: Monocot root: small pith; Dicot root: large pith
options.B: Dicot root: small/inconspicuous pith; Monocot root: large well-developed pith

questions.explanation: In dicot root, pith is small or inconspicuous. In monocot root, pith is large and well developed.

questions.question: Trichomes are described as being on:
questions.explanation: On the stem the epidermal hairs are called trichomes.

questions.question: Why is the study of plant anatomy useful?

options.B: It helps understand internal structure, function and adaptations

questions.explanation: Anatomy helps understand internal structure, functional organisation and adaptations to diverse environments.

questions.question: Epidermal cells are elongated and form:
questions.explanation: Epidermis is made up of elongated, compactly arranged cells, which form a continuous layer.

questions.question: Open vascular bundles are found in:
questions.explanation: In dicotyledonous stems, cambium is present between phloem and xylem. Such vascular bundles are called open vascular bundles.

questions.question: Where are root hairs functional in absorbing water?
questions.explanation: Root hairs are unicellular elongations of the epidermal cells and help absorb water and minerals from the soil.

questions.question: An isobilateral leaf can be identified by:

options.A: Presence of stomata only on the abaxial surface
options.B: Differentiated mesophyll with palisade and spongy layers
options.C: Stomata on both surfaces and undifferentiated mesophyll
options.D: Presence of bulliform cells on abaxial surface

questions.explanation: In an isobilateral leaf, the stomata are present on both the surfaces of the epidermis; and the mesophyll is not differentiated into palisade and spongy parenchyma.

questions.question: Which part of the plant does NOT have stomata?
questions.explanation: Stomata are present in the epidermis of leaves. Roots lack stomata and cuticle.

questions.question: In which type of vascular bundle is cambium absent?
questions.explanation: In monocotyledons, the vascular bundles have no cambium present in them, hence they are referred to as closed.

questions.question: What is the main function of root hairs?

options.B: Absorbing water and minerals from soil

questions.explanation: Root hairs are unicellular elongations of the epidermal cells and help absorb water and minerals from the soil.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the pericycle of a dicot root?

options.A: Thin-walled single layer next to epidermis
options.B: Few layers of thick-walled parenchyomatous cells next to endodermis
options.C: Multi-layered sclerenchymatous tissue in the cortex

questions.explanation: Next to endodermis lies a few layers of thick-walled parenchyomatous cells referred to as pericycle.

questions.question: In monocot stem, which feature is absent that is present in dicot stem?

options.C: Ring arrangement of vascular bundles and cambium in bundles

questions.explanation: In monocot stem, vascular bundles are scattered (not in ring) and they lack cambium (closed bundles).

questions.question: Phloem in a radial vascular bundle (roots) is arranged:

options.A: On the outer side of xylem
options.B: Alternately with xylem along different radii

questions.explanation: In radial arrangement, xylem and phloem are arranged alternately along different radii.

questions.question: Lateral roots arise from which tissue?
questions.explanation: Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium during the secondary growth takes place in pericycle cells.

questions.question: If a transverse section of a root shows a large pith and more than six xylem bundles, it is a:
questions.explanation: Monocot root has more than six (polyarch) xylem bundles and large, well-developed pith.

questions.question: If a transverse section of a stem shows scattered vascular bundles surrounded by sclerenchymatous bundle sheaths with absent phloem parenchyma, it is:
questions.explanation: This describes a monocot stem: scattered vascular bundles, sclerenchymatous bundle sheath, absent phloem parenchyma.

questions.question: If a transverse section of a stem shows ring-arranged conjoint open vascular bundles with endarch protoxylem and a large pith, it is:
questions.explanation: This describes a dicot stem: ring arrangement, conjoint open (with cambium), endarch protoxylem, large pith.

questions.question: Water loss in leaves is prevented by:
questions.explanation: The outside of the epidermis is often covered with a waxy thick layer called the cuticle which prevents the loss of water.

questions.question: Which of these tissues provides mechanical support specifically to the 'young' dicot stem?
questions.explanation: The outer hypodermis consists of a few layers of collenchymatous cells just below the epidermis, which provide mechanical strength to the young stem.

questions.question: The central parenchymatous mass in dicot stem is:
questions.explanation: A large number of rounded, parenchymatous cells with large intercellular spaces which occupy the central portion of the stem constitute the pith.

questions.question: In a dorsiventral (dicot) leaf, the spongy parenchyma is located:

options.A: On the upper side, below the adaxial epidermis
options.B: Below the palisade, above the abaxial epidermis

questions.explanation: The spongy parenchyma is situated below the palisade cells and extends to the lower (abaxial) epidermis.

questions.question: The term 'conjoint' in vascular bundle means:

options.A: Xylem and phloem on alternate radii
options.B: Xylem and phloem jointly along the same radius
options.C: No cambium between xylem and phloem

questions.explanation: In conjoint type, the xylem and phloem are jointly situated along the same radius of vascular bundles.

questions.question: Which structure allows grass leaves to respond to water stress by rolling?
questions.explanation: Bulliform cells in grasses, when flaccid due to water stress, make the leaves curl inwards to minimise water loss.

questions.question: Which of these features is NOT found in dicot stem?

options.C: Sclerenchymatous bundle sheath around each vascular bundle

questions.explanation: Sclerenchymatous bundle sheath surrounding each vascular bundle is a feature of monocot stem, not dicot stem.

questions.question: The suberin deposition in endodermis is water-impermeable. This is important for controlling:

options.B: Passage of water into the stele

questions.explanation: Casparian strips with water-impermeable suberin regulate the passage of water and dissolved substances into the stele.

questions.question: The ground tissue of monocot stem consists mainly of:
questions.explanation: The monocot stem has a large, conspicuous parenchymatous ground tissue.

questions.question: Which two tissues together constitute vascular bundles?
questions.explanation: The xylem and phloem together constitute vascular bundles.

questions.question: Which of the following is found in the pericycle of a dicot root but NOT in the epidermis?

options.B: Ability to initiate lateral roots and vascular cambium

questions.explanation: Initiation of lateral roots and vascular cambium during the secondary growth takes place in pericycle cells.

questions.question: Which of the following is a distinguishing feature of monocot root vs dicot root?

options.C: Polyarch xylem (more than 6) and large pith

questions.explanation: Monocot root has more than six (polyarch) xylem bundles and large, well-developed pith, unlike dicot root.

questions.question: In a dicot leaf, the upper epidermis (adaxial) generally:

options.B: Has fewer or even no stomata compared to lower epidermis

questions.explanation: The abaxial epidermis generally bears more stomata than the adaxial epidermis. The adaxial epidermis may even lack stomata.

questions.question: Cortex in dicot root is composed of which type of tissue?
questions.explanation: The cortex consists of several layers of thin-walled parenchyma cells with intercellular spaces.

questions.question: Which of the following is a distinguishing feature of dicot stem from monocot stem?

options.C: Presence of medullary rays between ring-arranged vascular bundles

questions.explanation: Medullary rays in between ring-arranged vascular bundles is a characteristic of dicot stem.

questions.question: Which part of the dicot stem has large intercellular spaces that occupy the central portion?
questions.explanation: A large number of rounded, parenchymatous cells with large intercellular spaces which occupy the central portion of the stem constitute the pith.

questions.question: In a T.S. of dicot stem, which of the following is the correct position of pericycle?

options.C: Inner side of endodermis, above phloem as semi-lunar sclerenchyma patches

questions.explanation: Pericycle is present on the inner side of the endodermis and above the phloem in the form of semi-lunar patches of sclerenchyma.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the stele in roots?
questions.explanation: Stele consists of all tissues on the innerside of the endodermis: pericycle, vascular bundles and pith. Cortex is outside the endodermis.

questions.question: The statement 'plants have cells as the basic unit, cells are organised into tissues and tissues into organs' reflects the concept of:
questions.explanation: This reflects the hierarchical organisation: cells → tissues → organs in plants.

questions.question: Bundle sheath cells in dicot leaf are:

options.C: Sclerenchymatous cells forming the whole leaf boundary

questions.explanation: The vascular bundles are surrounded by a layer of thick walled bundle sheath cells.

questions.question: The stomatal pore is enclosed by:
questions.explanation: Each stoma is composed of two bean-shaped cells known as guard cells which enclose stomatal pore.

questions.question: Which chapter topic deals with the comparison of monocot and dicot internal organisation?

options.B: Anatomy of Dicotyledonous and Monocotyledonous Plants

questions.explanation: Section 6.2 of the chapter is titled 'Anatomy of Dicotyledonous and Monocotyledonous Plants'.

questions.question: In a transverse section of a dicot root, if cambium ring has developed, it means:
questions.explanation: Later, a cambium ring develops between the xylem and phloem in dicot root, marking the beginning of secondary growth.

questions.question: The sclerenchymatous hypodermis in monocot stem compared to collenchymatous hypodermis in dicot stem differs in that:

options.A: Sclerenchyma is living, collenchyma is dead
options.B: Sclerenchyma is dead and rigid, collenchyma is living and flexible
options.C: Sclerenchyma has chloroplasts, collenchyma does not

questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma cells are typically dead and rigid (thick walls with lignin), while collenchyma cells are living with unevenly thickened walls — both provide support.

questions.question: Which section of the chapter discusses the isobilateral leaf?
questions.explanation: Section 6.2.6 discusses the Isobilateral (Monocotyledonous) Leaf.

questions.question: Which section of the chapter discusses the dorsiventral leaf?
questions.explanation: Section 6.2.5 discusses the Dorsiventral (Dicotyledonous) Leaf.

questions.question: The word 'endarch' refers to the position of protoxylem. 'Endarch' means protoxylem is:
questions.explanation: Endarch protoxylem means protoxylem is towards the centre (inside). In dicot stem, each vascular bundle has endarch protoxylem.

questions.question: Exarch protoxylem (protoxylem towards periphery) is found in:
questions.explanation: In roots, the arrangement is radial with exarch xylem (protoxylem facing towards outside). Dicot stem has endarch protoxylem.

questions.question: The epidermis of the dicot stem may bear:
questions.explanation: Covered with a thin layer of cuticle, the dicot stem epidermis may bear trichomes and a few stomata.

questions.question: The term 'radial' for a vascular bundle type means:

options.A: Xylem and phloem are on the same radius
options.B: Xylem and phloem alternate along different radii

questions.explanation: When xylem and phloem within a vascular bundle are arranged in an alternate manner along different radii, the arrangement is called radial.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct description of pericycle in dicot stem vs dicot root?

options.A: Both have same type: thick-walled parenchyma
options.B: Dicot stem: semi-lunar sclerenchyma patches above phloem; Dicot root: few layers of thick-walled parenchyoma
options.D: Dicot root: sclerenchyma patches; Dicot stem: parenchyma

questions.explanation: In dicot stem, pericycle appears as semi-lunar patches of sclerenchyma above phloem. In dicot root, it is a few layers of thick-walled parenchyomatous cells.

questions.question: The term 'bulliform' literally refers to cells that are:
questions.explanation: Bulliform cells are large, empty, colourless cells (bulla = bubble in Latin) found in grass leaves.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct match: tissue → location?

options.A: Medullary rays → Monocot stem between vascular bundles
options.B: Medullary rays → Dicot stem between vascular bundles
options.C: Medullary rays → Dicot root between cortex and endodermis

questions.explanation: Medullary rays are radially placed parenchymatous cells between vascular bundles in the dicot stem.

questions.question: In monocot stem, water-containing cavities are found:
questions.explanation: Water-containing cavities are present within the vascular bundles of monocot stem.

questions.question: Which of the following is an adaptation of the leaf mesophyll for efficient photosynthesis?

options.B: Palisade parenchyma with elongated cells vertically arranged for light absorption

questions.explanation: Palisade parenchyma on the adaxial side, with elongated cells arranged vertically and parallel, is adapted for maximum light absorption for photosynthesis.

questions.question: Which statement is correct about the dicot root vs monocot root?

options.A: Dicot root: polyarch; Monocot root: tetrarch/diarch
options.B: Monocot root: polyarch (>6 xylem bundles); Dicot root: fewer (2-4 xylem bundles)
options.C: Both: exactly same number of xylem bundles
options.D: Dicot root: no pericycle; Monocot root: pericycle present

questions.explanation: Monocot root has more than six (polyarch) xylem bundles; dicot root has usually two to four xylem and phloem patches.

questions.question: The function of the cuticle on the leaf epidermis is:
questions.explanation: The cuticle prevents the loss of water from the leaf surface.

questions.question: Which of the following shows the correct hierarchy of plant organisation as mentioned in the chapter?
questions.explanation: Plants have cells as the basic unit, cells are organised into tissues and in turn the tissues are organised into organs.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs is correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Radial vascular bundles are found in roots, where xylem and phloem alternate along different radii.

questions.question: Which of the following is a feature that distinguishes a monocot stem from dicot stem, as stated in the chapter?

options.A: Presence of epidermis in monocot stem
options.B: Phloem parenchyma absent in monocot stem
options.C: Presence of pith in dicot stem
options.D: Presence of cortex in dicot stem

questions.explanation: Phloem parenchyma is absent in monocot stem — this is specifically mentioned as a characteristic.

questions.question: What forms the 'stele' in roots?

options.C: Pericycle, vascular bundles and pith (all inside endodermis)

questions.explanation: All tissues on the innerside of the endodermis such as pericycle, vascular bundles and pith constitute the stele.

questions.question: Dorsiventral leaf has its name because:

options.A: Both dorsal and ventral sides are structurally identical
options.B: The dorsal (upper/adaxial) and ventral (lower/abaxial) sides are structurally different

questions.explanation: Dorsiventral leaf has structurally different upper (adaxial) and lower (abaxial) surfaces — more stomata on abaxial, differentiated mesophyll.

questions.question: Isobilateral leaf has its name because:

options.B: Both sides (surfaces) are structurally similar with stomata on both
options.C: It has bilateral symmetry in cross-section
options.D: It has one type of cell on each side

questions.explanation: Isobilateral leaf has stomata on both surfaces of the epidermis, making both sides structurally similar.

questions.question: The presence of which feature is used to identify if a stem section is monocot or dicot?

options.C: Arrangement of vascular bundles (scattered vs ring)

questions.explanation: Ring arrangement of vascular bundles = dicot stem; scattered vascular bundles = monocot stem.

questions.question: Sub-stomatal cavity is:

options.A: A space inside the guard cell
options.B: An air space below the stomatal pore in the mesophyll

questions.explanation: Sub-stomatal cavity is an air space just below the stomata in the leaf, visible in leaf transverse sections.

questions.question: The function of air cavities in spongy parenchyma of leaves is primarily:
questions.explanation: The large spaces and air cavities between spongy parenchyma cells facilitate gaseous exchange needed for photosynthesis and respiration.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs is INCORRECT?

options.D: Monocot stem: no cambium in vascular bundles

questions.explanation: Scattered vascular bundles are a characteristic of monocot stem, NOT dicot stem. Dicot stem has ring arrangement.

questions.question: Which of the following is correct about secondary growth?

options.A: It occurs in monocot stems only
options.B: It occurs in monocot roots only
options.C: It occurs in most dicotyledonous roots and stems
options.D: It occurs equally in monocots and dicots

questions.explanation: The secondary growth occurs in most of the dicotyledonous roots and stems.

questions.question: The term 'anatomy' in context of plants means:
questions.explanation: Study of internal structure of plants is called anatomy.

questions.question: Which of the following CORRECTLY summarises the three tissue systems of flowering plants?
questions.explanation: The three tissue systems are: epidermal (outermost), ground (main bulk of plant), and vascular (conducting tissue).

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct distinction between open and closed vascular bundles?

options.A: Open: no cambium; Closed: has cambium
options.B: Open: has cambium, forms secondary tissues; Closed: no cambium, no secondary growth
options.C: Both have cambium but only open forms secondary tissues

questions.explanation: Open vascular bundles have cambium and can form secondary xylem and phloem. Closed vascular bundles have no cambium and do not form secondary tissues.
